entry.title: Re: How can I find the source field of a target field in...
entry.content:
Hi,

The ‘VAT Registration No.’ field in the billing document header is sourced from the business partner master data. Specifically, it is derived from the VAT registration number maintained for the business partner Poland - Maintaining VAT Registration Number for Business Partners in the ‘Manage Business Partner Master Data’ app.

Source Field

  • Source Field: The VAT registration number is stored in the business partner master data under the ‘Tax Numbers’ section.

Business Partner Role

  • Business Partner Role: The VAT registration number can be associated with different roles such as Payer, BillTo, or ShipTo. The determination of which role’s VAT registration number is used can depend on the configuration settings for the sales organization and the specific sales document.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: Re: How to Use Vendor Sub-Ranges in SAP MM for Multi-Currency Procurement
entry.content:
Hi Leelamohan, 

Thanks for the explanation, but I must say that I disagree with your logic. The vendor subrange is not really meant for this. It helps you to divide the vendor's product range. Buying in different currencies can be dealt with in a much more automated way. You can maintain vendor-material inforecords on plant level, for example and the price on that same level. In that case everything will be determined automatically. It is also crucial to maintain your purchasing organisations on the right level. If a company has operational procurement locally but strategical procurement globally, then you should maintain a Purchase organisation locally for buying and a global one for pricing, where the global prices and contracts can be used by the local entity. This delivers much more automation, but also detailed process information. It will still allow you to use the same vendor master for all procurement. 

Kind regards, 

Sjaak

entry.title: Re: Do materials involved in third-party transactions nee...
entry.content:
Hi,

In SAP S/4HANA, maintaining the gross weight for materials is often required Supply Chain - Packaging Material for various processes, including third-party transactions. This requirement is typically due to the need for accurate logistics and transportation management, where the gross weight plays a crucial role Transportation Management ™ - Verified Gross Mass for a Container in determining shipping costs and compliance with regulations.

Is It Mandatory?

Yes, maintaining the gross weight for materials used in third-party processes is generally mandatory. This is because the gross weight is essential for calculating freight charges and ensuring proper handling during transportation. The system uses this information to manage logistics efficiently and comply with international shipping standards.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: How to change the pk naming convention to <table name>_PK
entry.content:
Hello

In SQL preview, the PK naming convention is not what expected.  it looks like:

constraint PK_<table name> primary key (<list of columns>)

How can I change the pk naming convention to <table name>_PK?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to change the pk naming convention to <table name...
entry.content:
Hello,

Please follow these steps:

  1. Select Database > Edit Current DBMS.
  2. Select ::Script\Objects\PKey\ConstName.
  3. Change the default value PK_%.U27:TABLE%
    to %.U27:TABLE%_PK
  4. Click OK to save and close the DBMS resource file.

Kind regards

entry.title: Utilization of SAP Integration suite to incorporate satellite IOT data into SAP Systems.
entry.content:
Hi All,

We can be able to integrate SAP Integration suite with the satellite IoT Data Providers which is an innovative as well as an emerging concept that has vast applications in logistics, defense and agriculture. It involves capturing of the data from satellites which includes geolocation, weather and asset movement while integrating it into the SAP systems like S/4 Hana, EWM and TM via SAP Integration Suite.


USECASE:

Imagining a logistics company with the fleets operating in some remote areas where GSM/4G signals are not available. Now, satellite IoT devices are often installed on the devices that could send telemetry data like location, temperature via satellite to a cloud provider or ground station. SAP Integration suite can then
ingest, process and roue this data into the SAP backend systems.

Architecture Overview:

 

Technical Flow:

i-Flow Design Steps in SAP Integration Suite:​

  1. Sender: HTTP Receiver from cloud IoT platform with the JSON payload.​
  2. Content Modifier: Extracting GPS, temperature as well as timestamp​
  3. Router: For applying the conditional logic.​
  4. Mapping: Conversion of the data type formats which can include JSON-> XML or it can be JSON-> SAP OData Format.​
  5. Receiver: Pushes data  into SAP backend using HTTP or OData destination.​

TESTING:​

We can simulate IoT platform using tools like Postman.

Conclusion:​

This integration would open doors to the smarter asset tracking as well as real-time decision making areas where GSM (Global System for Mobile Communication) is not available.  Hence, SAP Integration Suite makes it possible to diminish the gap between remote IoT data as well as the enterprise systems.

entry.title: Reusable SAP CPI Interface for Proxies Using Groovy: Simple Proxy XML to JSON Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog will show you how to create a single interface in SAP CPI that can be reused for processing proxy XML data from SAP S4/SAP systems or any other systems also.

Use Case

We will come across situations where we need to send different sets of data with different XML structures from SAP or SAP S4 to a target application using SAP CPI.

Normally, we design a specific proxy structure for XML that the ABAP team can use to send data to the middleware. But when there are multiple XML structures and the data needs to be sent separately from the source system, we often need to create multiple proxy structures. Also, if a field name needs to be changed or a new field needs to be added, the interface must be updated each time.

I came across this situation where SAP needed to send different XML messages, and the ABAP team asked for multiple proxy structures and changes. Each different message was going to the same target application but with a different resource path. This is going to take lot of time and different interfaces are needed as each message use different path in the HTTP.

To avoid this, I used a standardized XML format with simple <FieldName> and <FieldValue> pairs, and I added a resource path identifier in the header. This way, I was able to create just one reusable interface in SAP CPI that could handle all the different incoming messages, which I then sent to the target application.

Architecture

Development Steps

 

  1. Standardize Input XML in SAP

I have created Standardize SAP proxy structures into a common, uniform format with header

<FieldName> and <FieldValue> XML pair structure

 

This contains Header section I will be using to identify my resource path

In SAP CPI , I have used content modifier to capture the proxy header table data and saved it in property to use in HTTP receiver adapter. This property dynamically call the endpoint.

 

  1. Groovy Script in CPI for XML to Json

This Groovy script parses the incoming XML, extracts field-value pairs, and builds a JSON structure:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import java.util.HashMap;
import groovy.xml.*
import groovy.json.*
def Message processData(Message message)
{
    def xml = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
    def records = new XmlSlurper().parseText(xml)
    def jsonList = records.Record.collect { record ->
    def map = [:]
    record.Item.each { item ->
        map[item.FieldName.text()] = item.FieldValue.text()
    }
    return map
    }
    def json = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(jsonList))
    message.setBody(json)
    return message;
}

 

  1.  Post Transformed JSON Data to Target Endpoint

    HTTP Adapter configuration: In previous Content Modifier I have used Tablename to dynamically identify target URL.

     

     

     

Testing

Input XML1

<message>
	<Record>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>MANDT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>300</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>CLINT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>1234</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>KLART</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>003</FieldValue>
		</Item>
	</Record>
</message>

Output Json1:

[
	{
		"MANDT": "300",
		"CLINT": "1234",
		"KLART": "003"
	}
]

example2:

Input XML

<message>
	<Record>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>MANDT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>300</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>TPLNR</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>001A</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>SPRAS</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>E</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXTValueRecord1</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXU</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXUTValueRec1</FieldValue>
		</Item>
	</Record>
	<Record>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>MANDT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>300</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>TPLNR</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>001B</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>SPRAS</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>E</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXTValueRecord2</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXU</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXUTValueRec2</FieldValue>
		</Item>
	</Record>
</message>

OutputJson:

[
    {
        "MANDT": "300",
        "TPLNR": "001A",
        "SPRAS": "E",
        "PLTXT": "CheckPLTXTValueRecord1",
        "PLTXU": "CheckPLTXUTValueRec1"
    },
    {
        "MANDT": "300",
        "TPLNR": "001B",
        "SPRAS": "E",
        "PLTXT": "CheckPLTXTValueRecord2",
        "PLTXU": "CheckPLTXUTValueRec2"
    }
]

 

Take Away

  1. This design reusable, scalable integration strategy in SAP CPI using Groovy scripting and a generalized XML structure for simple transmissions.
  2. Implementing a single interface capable of handling multiple tables data with dynamic fields, this solution simplifies integration architecture, accelerates delivery, and reduces ongoing maintenance.
  3. Finally it eliminates the need of create multiple interfaces for similar data transmissions if we could able to handle simple design approcahes in SAP CPI.

entry.title: Re: Sales return generating multiple Nota Fiscal
entry.content:
Hi,

When you perform a sales return transaction with ‘Refund Type: Credit Memo’ and ‘Refund Control: Create Credit Memo’, the system is expected to generate a credit memo for the returned items. However, if a correction invoice is generated unexpectedly, it might be due to specific configurations Processing Return with a Customer Nota Fiscal Avulsa (NFA-e) or processes related to the handling of credit memos and returns.

Possible Causes:

  1. Configuration Settings: The system might be configured to automatically generate correction invoices under certain conditions, such as discrepancies in pricing or quantity.
  2. Process Flow: The creation of a correction invoice could be part of a standard process flow for handling returns and refunds, Claims, Returns, and Refund Management especially if there are adjustments needed for the original transaction.

Steps to Resolve:

  • Review Configuration: Check the configuration settings related to credit memo processing and returns to ensure they align with your expectations.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: SAP SAC Intgerated Financial Planning Calculating product costs
entry.content:
Hello All,

I'm trying to calculate the product cost rate in SAC but haven’t been able to get any results. I have four products — Product A, Product B, Product C, and Product D.

  • Product A and Product B are tagged to local sales GL accounts,

  • While Product B and Product C are tagged to export sales GL accounts,

  • This classification is based on the Distribution Channel property, where:

    • 10 = Local

    • 20 = Export

Each product has its own COGS values stored in the relevant leaf members of the following GL accounts:

  • 50301000 – COGS Direct Material

  • 50302000 – COGS Third Party

  • 50304000 – COGS Personnel Time

  • 50305000 – COGS Machine Time

  • 50307000 – COGS Production Overhead

Below is the code I'm executing to calculate product cost rate:

IF [d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT] = BASEMEMBER([d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT], "22") THEN // Gross Revenue
  DATA([d/Measures] = "QUANTITY", [d/Date] = #DATE) =
  RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "QUANTITY", [d/Version] = "public.Actual", [d/Date] = PREVIOUS(12)) *-1
ENDIF
 
IF [d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT] = BASEMEMBER([d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT], "21") THEN // COGS
DATA([d/Measures] = #AMOUNT, [d/Date] = #DATE) =
RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "AMOUNT", [d/Version] = "public.Actual",  [d/Date] = PREVIOUS(12))
ENDIF
 
IF [d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT] = BASEMEMBER([d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT], "21") THEN // COGS
DATA([d/Measures] = "PRICE") =
RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = #AMOUNT, [d/Date] = #DATE) 
/ RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "QUANTITY", [d/Date] = #DATE)
 
ENDIF

 

Despite this logic, no results are returned, and I’m unsure why. Could someone please help identify the issue or suggest the correct approach?

 

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Custom Widgets in SAP Analytics Cloud - Increased flexibility for Reporting and Planning
entry.content:
What is a SAC Custom Widget? 

The SAP Analytics Cloud offers companies a powerful platform for business intelligence, planning and predictive analyses. SAC provides a large number of standard widgets - so-called basic widgets - with which reports and dashboards can be designed quickly and effectively. Even though these basic widgets cover many common requirements and support a total of 21 different chart types, users sometimes come up against limitations when it comes to more specific visualization requirements. 

This is exactly where custom widgets come into play: these are individually developed visualization elements that go beyond what SAP offers as standard. A SAC custom widget is essentially a web component that consists of a JSON file for the description, JavaScript code for the behavior and optional additional resources such as CSS or images. This allows new chart types as well as customized user interface elements, interactions or even completely new functionalities to be implemented. 

 

GRAPHIC 1: EXAMPLE OF A CUSTOM WIDGET

 

 

Custom widgets offer developers full control: they can design their own diagrams and visualizations from scratch that are perfectly tailored to the specific requirements of a company - be it an interactive network diagram, a dynamic map view or a customized user interface. Thanks to the integration in SAC Stories and the support of the script API, custom widgets fit seamlessly into the existing environment and can be conveniently integrated into Stories via drag & drop. 

Custom widgets therefore allow the SAP Analytics Cloud to be expanded considerably - both in terms of visual design and functional depth. They are the ideal tool if the standard options are not sufficient and the focus is on individual visualization requirements. 

Application scenarios for custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud 

Custom widgets open up a wide range of possible applications in the SAP Analytics Cloud, especially when standard widgets are not sufficient to meet specific requirements. A key area of application is the development of tailor-made visualizations that go beyond the 21 chart types available in the standard system. 

 

GRAPHIC 2: CUSTOM WIDGETS ARE AS INDIVIDUAL AS YOUR COMPANY'S REQUIREMENTS

 

For example, companies can create their own KPI tiles that contain numerical values as well as contextual information such as deviations or trend indicators. In addition, custom widgets enable the integration of interactive user interface elements such as calendars, date pickers or barcode generators that are required in specific business processes. The integration of SAPUI5 controls is also possible, allowing existing components to be reused and complex applications to be implemented efficiently. 

Another scenario is the extension of the SAC functionality by integrating external data sources or APIs. For example, custom widgets can visualize data from third-party systems in real time or enable specific interactions that cannot be realized with standard widgets. This offers companies the flexibility to tailor their dashboards and analyses precisely to their individual needs.  

Overall, custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud offer a powerful way to extend standard functionalities and efficiently implement individual requirements. They are particularly advantageous when specific visualizations, user interactions or data integrations are required that go beyond the standard. 

Custom widgets in detail - how they work in the SAP Analytics Cloud 

Custom widgets can be seamlessly integrated into SAP Analytics Cloud Stories and behave like any other standard widget. After successful deployment, they are automatically listed in the widget menu so that they can be dragged and dropped onto the canvas and placed there. Once inserted, custom widgets can be flexibly moved, resized and visually designed - just like native components in SAC. 

 

GRAPHIC 3: ADD CUSTOM WIDGETS

 

Developers and users benefit from a wide range of functions. Basic properties such as color, font or frame can be adjusted via the styling panel. What is particularly interesting is that in the “Additional properties” section, you can add your own options that have been specially defined for the respective custom widget. Additional settings and functions can be stored in the builder panel, for example for data binding or initializing interactions. 

Custom widgets also support direct data binding, which means that they can be connected to data sources - a key requirement for interactive and data-driven visualizations. In the widget structure within a SAC story, custom widgets appear like any other component and can therefore be easily organized and targeted. 

Another powerful feature is the scripting capability: custom widgets can be addressed via the familiar SAC script API. In addition to the methods available as standard, you can also define and implement your own events - such as an onClick that triggers certain actions when the widget is clicked. This flexibility makes Custom Widgets a versatile tool that can be deeply integrated into SAC, both visually and functionally. 

Technical requirements and authorizations 

The development and use of custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud requires specific technical prerequisites and authorizations. By default, SAP roles are restricted to read access. To create or update custom widgets, users must be assigned additional authorizations. These authorizations can either be set globally for all custom widgets or individually per widget. In particular, widget designers require the rights to create and update widgets. 

Several files are required to create a custom widget, including a JSON file to define the metadata and a JavaScript file for the functionality. These files are packed into a ZIP file together with optional resources such as CSS files or images. This ZIP file is then uploaded directly to the SAC, eliminating the need for external hosting. 

The development environment for custom widgets includes tools such as Visual Studio Code, a free editor from Microsoft that is particularly suitable for editing HTML and JavaScript. To create complex charts, developers can integrate free chart libraries such as ECharts, D3.js or Chart.js to realize advanced visualization options. 

 

GRAPHIC 4: DEVELOPMENT ENVIRONMENT

 

However, there are currently certain limitations when using custom widgets. For example, only Chromium-based browsers such as Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge are fully supported. In addition, the number of custom widgets that can be loaded into a single SAC tenant is limited to 50. 

Some features of the SAC are not supported by custom widgets. These include themes and CSS adjustments, pause refresh, commenting, scheduling, translations, data change insights and the “Search to Insight” function. Restrictions may also occur when exporting custom widgets to PDF, especially if certain chart libraries such as D3.js are used or if CSS settings are defined in the widget. 

There are numerous examples for references in the SAP Community and in SAP Help: 

  • SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget Developer Guide 
  • SAP Custom Widget examples on GitHub 
  • SAP Technology Blog: Custom Widgets & Widget Add-Ons 
  • Echarts library examples 
  • SAP Analytics Cloud Widget Add-On Developer Guide 
     

Your path to SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widgets 

The introduction of custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud offers companies an outstanding opportunity to expand their analytics solutions individually and to tailor them visually and functionally to specific requirements. To maximize the benefits, we recommend a structured and proven approach. 

  1. requirements analysis: In initial workshops, we work with you to analyze the technical, functional and content requirements for the planned custom widget. In this way, we ensure that all stakeholder needs are understood and taken into account - from IT to the specialist department. 
  2. proof of concept: We then develop an initial minimum viable product (MVP) to demonstrate the technical feasibility. This PoC serves as the basis for further decisions and for internal coordination with relevant teams. 
  3. implementation: Based on the PoC, we add the required functionalities to the widget, test it in your SAC environment and hand over the finished custom widget as part of a clean documentation and handover. 
  4.  

GRAPHIC 5: EVALUATION

 

Custom widgets are a modular building block for making a variety of advanced functions usable in SAC - from individual visualizations to complex interactions. They promote acceptance among end users and enable customized solutions for company-specific challenges. At the same time, you should consider the increased development effort compared to standard widgets and the possible training requirements for your team - because individual developments also require internal know-how in the long term.

entry.title: SAP Transportation Management for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition 2023 FPS02
entry.content:
With SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition 2023 FPS02, you can now activate TM scenarios as part of best practice content and jumpstart your implementation. These scenarios were available only for Public Cloud before this release.

Before going into details of these scenarios , lets have a quick look on what exactly is SAP Transportation Management.

SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM) supports you in all activities connected with the physical transportation of goods from one location to another. You can use SAP TM to perform the following activities, for example:

  • Create forwarding orders for your ordering parties
  • Transfer orders and deliveries from an ERP system
  • Create freight bookings
  • Plan the transportation and select carriers
  • Tender transportation services
  • Calculate the transportation charges for both the ordering party and the supplier side
  • Consider foreign trade and dangerous goods regulations

In the supply chain network, SAP TM helps the businesses to plan their transports by choosing the optimized mode of shipment, best carrier selection based on rating, cost, efficiency etc., thereby also choosing the optimized routes. 

SAP TM allows manual and automated Tracking & Tracing of shipments by monitoring and reporting events in real time.

Transportation Management System helps to improve operations, drive business, and provide better service to customers. In doing so, the TMS offers numerous benefits that help the company move freight from origin to destination accurately and cost-effectively.

Now lets look at the Available Scenarios in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud

There are total 6 scope items for Transportation Management. 3 of them are Advanced and  3 of them are labelled as Basic depending on the functionality and licensing cost.

SAP TM mainly is divided into Transportation Planning, Transportation Execution, Settlements and verifications.

 Advanced TM

  • 6W2 - Manual Transportation Planning || S4HANA PCE 2023 FPS02
  • 6W3 - Transportation Execution || S/4HANA PCE 2023 - FPS02
  • 6W4 - Freight Settlement || S/4HANA PCE 2023 - FPS02

Basic TM

  • 7IV - Manual Transportation Planning - Basic || S4HANA PCE 2023 FPS02
  • 7IW - Transportation Execution - Basic || S/4HANA PCE 2023 - FPS 02
  • 7IX - Freight Settlement - Basic || S/4HANA PCE 2023 - FPS 02

Manual Transportation Planning - 6W2/7IV

Manual transportation planning allows you to create or change a transportation plan. To create a transportation plan, you assign transportation demands (such as freight unit stages) to transportation capacities (such as equipment types, for example, truck types, rail car types, or container types). The system creates capacity documents (freight orders or freight bookings). Scheduling runs automatically after you perform a planning activity for a freight order. Manual planning is done in the Transportation Cockpit app.

Transportation Execution - 6W3 /7IW

 With this process, you execute and track the transportation process for your freight documents. You can set the relevant statuses to track the arrival and departure at your locations and set the checkout status to enable freight cost confirmation. You can set statuses for the entire document, as well as for individual stages and locations.

Freight Settlement - 6W4 /7IX

 Freight costs calculated for the freight documents (freight orders and freight bookings) are to be posted in financial accounting to record such costs in accounting as accruals and to pay the carriers or freight forwarders. Once the freight documents are in a status in which the transportation has been initiated or completed, the freight costs can be posted to financial accounting. If there are errors during the posting of accruals, the freight documents can be monitored for such posting errors. The invoice received from the carrier or freight forwarder is verified against the expected costs calculated in the freight document. Depending on the number of invoicing parties involved for a freight document, multiple invoices can be received from different carriers executing the transport. For example, for an ocean freight booking, you can receive invoices from different invoicing carriers to execute different stages of the ocean booking (for example, one for the pick-up, one for the main carriage, and another one for the delivery). During invoice verification, price variances and unplanned freight costs can be captured. Freight cost allocation capabilities are used to manage freight costs that occurred during the transportation processes. The costs are posted to a material valuation in an inbound process, and to an expense account (general ledger) in an outbound process.

Now since we understood ‘what’ of these scenarios , lets have a look at ‘How’

In order to initiate the TM processes, the pre requisite preceding documents need to be created, also referred to as ‘Business Conditions’

Based on this preceding document (Business Condition), the inbound or outbound flow will be executed. This part of the process can be executed with scope items 2TX, BDD, BD9 and 3NR, as shown in the diagram.

 

Inbound and Outbound flow can be executed for 4 modes of transport – Road, Rail, Ocean and Air.

All the succeeding documents will be created as you execute the process – Freight Unit, Freight Order (Rail and Road), Freight Booking (Ocean and Air) and Freight Settlement Document.

Inbound Scenario
The following end to end process Transportation Management inbound consists of the scope items 2TX (Direct Procurement with Inbound Delivery)  or BDD (2TX in this example)  , 6W2 Manual Transportation Planning, 6W3 Transportation Execution and 6W4 Freight Settlement.

 

Outbound Scenario
The following end to end process Transportation Management outbound consists of the scope items BD9 Sell from Stock or 3NR Sales Scheduling agreements, 6W2 Manual Transportation Planning, 6W3 Transportation Execution and 6W4 Freight Settlement.

 

. Difference between Basic and Advanced Scope Items-

  • For Advanced scenarios, Freight unit is created even before creating delivery . But for Basic scenarios, delivery needs to be created first to create Freight Unit
  • Advanced scenarios involve a licensing cost while Basic scenarios do not involve any additional cost

For further information on the topics , you can go through below links-

6W2 - https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-055/2023-FPS02/SolP/6W2/?region=DE

6W3 - https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-055/2023-FPS02/SolP/6W3/?region=DE

6W4 - https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-055/2023-FPS02/SolP/6W4/?region=DE

7IV - https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-055/2023-FPS02/SolP/7IV/?region=DE

7IW - https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-055/2023-FPS02/SolP/7IW/?region=DE

7IX - https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-055/2023-FPS02/SolP/7IX/?region=DE

Administration Guide to Implementation of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023 FP2 with SAP Best Practices

entry.title: SFTP Authentication Setup in SAP BTP Integration Suite: A Step-by-Step Guide.
entry.content:
The objective of this tutorial is to guide users through the step-by-step process of setting up SFTP authentication within an SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) landscape using SAP Integration Suite. The tutorial focuses on configuring secure key-based authentication by generating SSH key pairs, uploading keys to the SFTP server and BTP keystore, and integrating the configuration into an iFlow. 

 Access the SFTP Platform :-

  • Visit the URL:https://sftpcloud.io/tools/free-sftp-server
  • Click on the Login button located at the top right corner.

  Sign Up / Log In

  • Use the 'Sign Up / Log In' option to register or enter your account.

  Create SFTP instance

  • After a successful login, To Create New SFTP instance.

  • Next, Open the SFTP instance, click on 'Users', and then go to 'Connection Details’.

     After configure SFTP server, we will have some info of it as

  • User name
  • Password phrase
  • Host name
  • SFTP Port

   Install WinSCP

  • Visit the URL:https://winscp.net/eng/download.php
  • Open WinSCP and login using the Host Name, User Name, and Password from the SFTP server.

  • Here, we create a directory or file in WinSCP to upload or download files from the SFTP server.

    Create Known Host in CPI
  1. Go to integration Suite Application
  2. Go to Design integration flow
  3. Go to Monitor item in left menu
  4. Go to Connectivity Test

  • Open Notepad, paste the Host Key, and save the file with an appropriate name (e.g.,hostkey.txt).
  • Add this known host into CPI

    Create User Credentials

  1. Go to integration Suite Application
  2. Go to Design integration flow
  3. Go to Monitor item in left menu
  4. Go to Security Material
  5. Click on create

     To check the user credentials, go to 'Connectivity Test' and click on 'SSH'.

 

1. Username/Password :-

  • SFTP SENDER IFLOW

     Create/Configure an Integration Flow

  1. We are using HTTPS to send the data {\"productIdentifier\":\"HT-2000\"} because this value needs to be passed to the OData service.

     2. The JSON to XML Converter is used to convert incoming JSON data into XML, as required           by the OData service.

     3. Use the Content Modifier to assign information to headers.

     4. Use the Request-Reply step to fetch product details from the OData service.

     5. The OData service is being accessed from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com).

     In the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, navigate to APIs → ODATA V2, then search for 'Product  Master'.

      Click on "Show API Key" to get the key needed for authentication.

     In the API Specification, you can download the OData EDMX, which is useful when integrating in SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) for OData service modeling.

     In the Configuration Details section, the Sandbox URL helps in forming the complete endpoint URL by replacing the {host} placeholder with the actual hostname when configuring the OData connection in SAP Cloud Integration (CPI).

     6. Now in the connection, use the Sandbox URL as the Address.

     We are using the data {\"productIdentifier\":\"HT-2000\"} to retrieve the specific product details from the OData service.

     7. Next, use the XML to CSV Converter to send data to the receiver, as the SFTP server only accepts CSV format.

     8. The SFTP Adapter is configured at the receiver end to deliver the converted CSV file. The directory and address are taken from the SFTP server, the file name is created dynamically, and the credentials are retrieved from the security material.

     9. Next, save and deploy the integration. Then, open Manage Integration Content and copy the Endpoint URL.

     10. Go to Postman, select the GET method, and paste the endpoint URL.

     In the Auth type, select OAuth 2.0, and provide the Client ID, Client Secret, and Token URL from the service instance. Then, click Get New Access Token.

     Provide the body data, click on Send, and view the result.

     11. Now, go to WinSCP, where you can see the CSV file or data in the specified directory.

 

  • SFTP RECEIVER IFLOW

     Create/Configure an Integration Flow

  1. Open or create an Integration Flow (iFlow).
  2. Connect the sender participant to Start and select the SFTP as adapter. Configure the SFTP adapter as per requirements.

     Make sure that the SFTP credentials are deployed in the below path

     Overview --> Manage Security --> Security Material --> Create/Upload Credentials

     Check the connectivity test of SFTP adapter in Test Connectivity in the below path

     Overview --> Manage Security --> Connectivity Tests --> SSH

     Copy the Host Key and paste it in notepad & deploy it in Known Hosts(SSH) file in the below path.

      Overview --> Manage Security --> Security Material --> Upload Known Hosts(SSH)

     3. In the processing tab also configure as per your requirement whether you want to Archive, Keep File and Process Again.

     4. In the scheduler tab also we need to choose how many times this particular interface needs to be run.

     5. Use the CSV to XML Converter to transform the incoming CSV file into structured XML format.

     6. After saving and deploying the integration, go to Monitor → Message Processing →Message Processing Run, click on the end of the run to view the XML data.

 

2. PUBLIC KEY :-

  • Create SSH KEY :
  1. Go to Monitor > Integrations and APIs, open Manage Keystore, click Create, and select SSH Key to generate a new SSH key.

     2. After creating the SSH key, click on the three dots (︙) and select Download Public OpenSSH Key.

     Now open the SFTP server. In Windows PowerShell, run the following commands:

  • cd .\Downloads\ – to change the directory to Downloads
  • ls – to list the files and confirm your SSH key is there
  • cat <your_ssh_key_name>.pub – to display the public SSH key

     3. Copy the displayed public SSH key, open the SFTP server, navigate to Public Keys, paste the key, and click Save.

 

  • SFTP SENDER IFLOW

     Using the same flows, just changing the authentication method from username/password to private key.

  1. Open the receiver SFTP adapter, select 'Public Key' for authentication, and provide the private key alias and the user name from the SFTP server.

     2. Next, save and deploy the integration. Then, open Manage Integration Content and copy the Endpoint URL.

     3. In the Auth type, select OAuth 2.0, and provide the Client ID, Client Secret, and Token URL from the service instance. Then, click Get New Access Token. Provide the body data, click on Send, and view the result.

     4. Now, go to WinSCP, where you can see the CSV file or data in the specified directory.

 

  • SFTP RECEIVER IFLOW
  1. Open the sender SFTP adapter, select 'Public Key' for authentication, and provide the private key alias and the user name from the SFTP server.

     2. After saving and deploying the integration, go to Monitor → Message Processing →Message Processing Run, click on the end of the run to view the XML data.

 

3. DUAL :-

  • SFTP SENDER IFOW

     Using the same flows, just changing the authentication method to Dual.

  1. Open the receiver SFTP adapter, select 'Dual' for authentication, and provide the private key alias and the Credential name.

     2. Next, save and deploy the integration. Then, open Manage Integration Content and copy the Endpoint URL.

       3. In the Auth type, select OAuth 2.0, and provide the Client ID, Client Secret, and Token URL from the service instance. Then, click Get New Access Token. Provide the body data, click on Send, and view the result.

      4. Now, go to WinSCP, where you can see the CSV file or data in the specified directory.

  • SFTP RECEIVER IFOW

      Using the same flows, just changing the authentication method to Dual.

  1. Open the receiver SFTP adapter, select 'Dual' for authentication, and provide the private key alias and the Credential name.

     2. After saving and deploying the integration, go to Monitor → Message Processing →Message Processing Run, click on the end of the run to view the XML data.

 

The content of this blog is based on personal experience and is intended for educational and informational purposes only. The steps and configurations shared here are specific to the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and SAP Integration Suite and may vary depending on your organization’s security policies and infrastructure setup. Always follow your organization's guidelines and consult official SAP documentation when implementing security configurations or deploying to production environments. The author is not responsible for any issues arising from improper use or application of the information provided.

entry.title: Driving Authorization of a BW query in SAP BW/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction

Driving authorization of a BW query in SAP BW/4HANA involves controlling which users can access which data while executing the query. It ensures that users can only view the data they’re allowed to see based on security and authorization settings. This is typically done using Authorization Objects, Analysis Authorizations, and InfoObject restrictions.

Before we get into this topic, I recommend checking out my previous blog where I explained the BEx query elements, which will help you better understanding this blog.

The main focus of this blog is to cover the scenarios that are commonly encountered with respect to Authorization of a BW queries.

  1. How to identify which BW query/queries uses a specific authorization variable.
  2. How to identify which BW query/queries uses a specific InfoObject in fixed filter section of a query?
  3. Step-by-Step process to restrict BW Query Access by authorization relevant InfoObject.

Now let’s discuss one by one in detail.

  1. How to identify which BW query/queries uses a specific authorization variable

Let's assume we have an authorization variable called "0A_COSTC". We need to identify which BW queries uses this specific variable. This can be achieved by correlating RSZCOMPDIR, RSZELTXREF & RSZRANGE tables in the backend. This approach will help us understand the impact on a BW queries if we make any changes to the Variable or the associated InfoObject.

Step 1: In the table RSZCOMPDIR, enter the authorization variable name in the field COMPID to obtain COMPUID of a authorization variable and execute the table.Screenshot 1 : Input to RSZCOMPDIR table

Step 2: In the table RSZRANGE, enter the COMPUID of the authorization variable (from Step 1) in the field LOW to obtain ELTUID and execute the table.Screenshot 3 : Input to RSZRANGE tableScreenshot 4: Output of RSZRANGE table

Step 3: In the table RSZELTXREF, enter ELTUID (from step 2) in the field TELTUID to obtain SELTUID and execute the table.Screenshot 5: Input to RSZELTXREF tableScreenshot 6 : output of RSZELTXREF table

Step 4: Again, in the table RSZELTXREF, enter SELTUID (from step 3, where LAYTP = ‘AGG’) in the field TELTUID to obtain SELTUID and execute the tableScreenshot 7: Input to RSZELTXREFScreenshot 8: Output of RSZELTXREF table

Step 5:  In the table RSZCOMPDIR, enter SELTUID (from step 4, where LAYTP = ‘SOB’) in the field COMPUID to obtain BW query/queries list in which Authorization variable is used.Screenshot 9: Input to RSZCOMPDIR tableScreenshot 10: Output of RSZCOMPDIR table

Note: In step 5, we obtained the list of queries where the authorization (processing type) variable was used. Follow the same procedure to identify other type of variables used in BEx queries. 

2. How to identify which BW query/queries uses a specific InfoObject in fixed filter section of a BW query 

In BW queries, the Fixed Filter section is used to restrict data at the query level, meaning the filter is always applied and cannot be changed during query execution. Often, specific InfoObjects like Company Code, Cost Center or Region are used here to limit the data scope for reporting or security purposes.

For Example, let's say we have an InfoObject called "0COMP_CODE". This InfoObject might be used in the Fixed Filter section of certain BW queries to ensure that only data related to a specific company code is included in the report. If we plan to delete, rename, or change the behavior of the "0COMP_CODE" InfoObject, it is crucial to understand which queries will be affected.

Step 1:  In the table RSZRANZE, enter InfoObject name in the field IOBJNM to obtain ELTUID and execute table. Screenshot 11: Input to RSZRANGE tableScreenshot 12: Output of RSZRANGE table

Step 2: In the table RSZELTXREF, enter ELTUID (from step 1) in the field TELTUID to obtain SELTUID and execute the table.Screenshot 13: Input to RSZELTXREF tableScreenshot 14: Output of RSZELTXREF table

Step 3: Again, in the table RSZELTXREF, enter SELTUID (from step 2, where LAYTP = ‘AGG’) in the field TELTUID to obtain SELTUID and execute the table.Screenshot 15: Input to RSZELTXREF tableScreenshot 16: Output of RSZELTXREF table

Step 4: In the table RSZCOMPDIR, enter SELTUID (from step 3, where LAYTP = ‘SOB’) in the field COMPUID to obtain BW query/queries list in which specific InfoObject is used.Screenshot 17: Input to RSZCOMPDIR tableScreenshot 18: Output of RSZCOMPDIR table

Note: In step 4, we obtained the list of queries where the 0COMP_CODE InfoObject was used. Follow the same procedure to identify other  InfoObjects used in BW queries.

3. Step by step process to restrict BW query access by authorization relevant InfoObject

In most business scenarios, Company code is a critical organizational element used to separate financial data within an enterprise. To ensure data security and compliance, it is often necessary to restrict users access to BW queries based on their authorized company codes. This prevents users from viewing financial data for companies they are not responsible for or permitted to access.

For example, if a user is authorized to access only company code 1000, they should not be able to view reports that include data from company code 2000 or others. SAP BW enables such security control through the use of authorization objects, authorization variables and analysis authorizations (RSECADMIN).

By following a structured step by step process, we can ensure that users only see data relevant to their roles, thereby enforcing data-level security in BW reporting.

Step 1: Identify the InfoObject for Company Code

  • Usually, the InfoObject for Company Code is 0COMP_CODE.
  • Ensure this InfoObject is marked as authorization-relevant in its properties.

Screenshot 19: Display of InfoObject 0COMP_CODE

Step 2: Create or Maintain an Authorization variable

  • Go to HANA Studio and create a new authorization variable on 0COMP_CODE.
    • Example Variable: ZV_COMP_CODE
    • Processing Type: Authorization
    • Variable Represents: Single Value/Multiple values
    • Ready for Input: Optional (Depending on your use case)

GIF File: Creating variable

Step 3: Insert the Authorization Variable in BW Query Filter

  • Open Your BW Query in the HANA Studio.
  • In the Fixed Filter section, drag the Company code InfoObject.
  • Assign the Created Authorization variable (ZV_COMP_CODE) as the filter value.
  • This ensures the query filters Company Code values based on the User's authorizations.

Screenshot 20: Displaying Query with restriction on 0COMP_CODE in HANA studio

Step 4 : Maintain Analysis Authorizations

  • Go to Transaction : RSECADMIN.
  • Under the Authorizations tab, create or modify an authorization object.
    • Include InfoObject 0COMP_CODE with appropriate values (e.g., 1000, 2000, 3000).
    • Use Value "*" if access to all company codes is allowed.
  • Assign the authorization object to the user or role under the User tab.

Screenshot 21: Initial screen of RSECADMIN T-code

Step 5: Test with Different Users

  • Log in with different user IDs having different Company Code authorizations.
  • Run the BW query and verify that each user sees only the data for authorized Company Codes.

entry.title: Working with Feature & Transport Movement in SAP Cloud ALM: S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition
entry.content:
Objective: To provide a step-by-step guide on effectively working with feature in SAP Cloud ALM for S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE), including how to manage their lifecycle and align them with the transport movement process across the system landscape.
1. Creating a feature and key parts of a feature
2. Assigning/Creating a transport in a feature
3. Creating TOCs from Transports in a feature
4. Releasing and deploying Transports to Quality and Production system.


1. Creating a feature and key parts of a feature:

Login to the CALM tenant and click on Implementation->Projects & Setup:

If you're using an end-to-end process, you usually create features in the Requirements app.
You can also create a feature in the Features app:

 

1. Add a title for the feature in the Title field.
2. Add additional information in the text field.

3. In the Additional Information section, you can specify a feature for Context, Status and Planning, and Object:

As seen in the screenshot, you can also specify Release, requirement and Responsible user to a feature:

Requirement:
You can assign a requirement to a feature, so that it's possible to link features to existing requirements. Choose the respective requirement from the dropdown selection.

Release:

With the release assignment, you can add a release to your features.

Based on the assigned releases, you’re able to bundle features that should go-live together.

Assigning a deployment plan to a project in the Projects and Setup app in SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation, provides you with the option to assign a release to features.

Responsible User:

You can add a Responsible user to a feature. To achieve this, select a name from the Responsible dropdown.
If the responsible person has changed, you're notified by the Notifications &#xe23a functionality.

While working on feature management, we can specify user stories, references and project tasks as well but in this blog, we will discuss on assigning/creating Transports and moving the Transports to Quality and then to production in a landscape.

 

2. Assigning/Creating a transport in a feature:
If you have already created a Transport and added the changes to the Transport, you can directly assign these Transports to your Feature.
To assign a Transport, in the Transports section click on "Assign":

A popup with all the available Transports in your Development system appears, select the transport and assign it to the feature.
Note: If a Transport is already assigned to some other feature, it will not be available for any other feature to be selected.

To Create a Transport, Click on Create button in the feature on upper right corner and select Transport:

 

A pop-up to create a transport appears, you can select either workbench TR or Customizing or both:

A transport will be assigned/ created as per your wish in a feature in SAP CALM:

 

Save the Feature.


3. Creating TOCs from Transports in a feature:
What is a TOC:
In SAP Cloud ALM, a TOC (Transport of Copies) is a type of transport request used to move a snapshot of changes (without releasing the original transport) across the system landscape—mainly for testing or quality assurance purposes without affecting the main development flow. In SAP Cloud ALM, a TOC (Transport of Copies) is a type of transport request used to move a snapshot of changes (without releasing the original transport) across the system landscape—mainly for testing or quality assurance purposes without affecting the main development flow.
Once you assign/ Create Transports in the feature, click on start Implementation:


And confirm the pop-up:



The status of the Feature will change to "In Implementation"
Once you're ready with the changes in the transport, release the tasks of the transports assigned to the feature in the Development system:

Now come back to CALM tenant, in the Transports section there will be a button "Copy", this button is used to create TOCs for the Transports:

Click on the button, a pop-up with the TRs will appear, select the TRs for which you need to create TOCs and import TOCs to the Quality system:

Creation of TOCs will be scheduled, this button will not only create the TOCs but will also import the TOCs with the changes made in Development system to Quality system:
When you click on the history button, you will be able to the see the status for TOC creation:

Grey icon shows TOC creation is in progress:

Once the TOC creation is successful, the icon turns green, and the TOC is scheduled to Deploy in Quality system automatically:

TOC is then deployed to Quality system automatically, check the Quality system to verify if the changes are there in the Quality system:

The TOCs are moved to Quality, you need to change the status of the feature to "In Testing", to do that click on "Handover to Test" in feature:

The Feature is now in "In Testing".

4. Releasing and deploying Transports to Quality and Production system:
Once the Changes are moved to Quality in TOCs and they are tested in Quality, it's time to move the main TR to Quality. To do that click on the button release in Transports section in Feature:

 

 

You can go and check in the Development system, the TR gets released automatically.
Now, to deploy the TR in Quality, SAP CALM has a button "Deploy" in the Transports section beside "Release button", click on the button to Deploy the TR in Quality system:

 

The TR gets deployed in Quality system automatically.


For next steps on Configuring and Performing the Transport checks and deploying the TRs in Production check my next blog:
How to Configure and Perform Transport Checks in S... - SAP Community

Also, check my blog: Re: How to Configure SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation for Your SAP S/4 HANA Private Cloud Landscape if you want to configure SAP CALM for Implementation for S/4HANA Private Cloud Landscape.

entry.title: Re: Actual Activity Rate Calculation without using Materi...
entry.content:
Hi @KaranChauhan,

if you are not using actual costing, the delta between planned and actual activity prices are not allocated on the product/material level. You can use the actual activity prices to revaluate the allocations between cost centers with transaction/app KSII.

In S/4HANA On Premise/Private Cloud, you have the option to revaluate your production orders with the delta between these prices using transaction CON2. After allocating the delta to production order, the amount will be settled to a variance account via production order settlement. This functionality is currently not available in Public Cloud. While it was initially out of scope for Public Cloud, we are currently investigating how and when to make a functionality like this available in Public Cloud as well. I cannot promise you any specific functionality and timeline right now.

I hope this clarifies the situation. If this is the case, please accept this answer to update the status of this thread accordingly.

Thanks and best regards
Marvin

entry.title: Re: Error while adding a goal - Cannot set properties of...
entry.content:
Hi Jayesh, interestingly I had a user report this issue this morning and stumbled upon this post when I searched in SAP for Me. The user in question does have the Goal Management Access RBP. Like you say, it's very hard to troubleshoot as if they click out and load up their goal plan again, they can create the goal without issue. And then at some point later when trying to create another goal, the problem comes back again.

This isn't something I've seen before (and we've been using Latest Goal Management for a couple of years now). I am going to raise a support case and hopefully SAP will then see that something needs to be investigated.

entry.title: Implementing Event-Driven Architecture with Canonical Data Model in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA):
Event-Driven Architecture is a design pattern in which system components communicate by producing and consuming events. An event represents a change of state (e.g., a new sales order, product update). In EDA, producers send events without knowing who consumes them, enabling loose coupling between systems.

Key benefits:

  • Decoupling

  • Real-time data flow

  • Scalability and flexibility

Canonical Data Model (CDM):
A Canonical Data Model is a standardized format for data exchange between systems. Instead of each system mapping data differently, a single unified model is used across all integrations.

The Integration Flow implementation looks like this:

  • Step 1: Prepare Canonical Data

  • Prepare your canonical XML/JSON payload. Here’s a sample XML code:

     

<Root>
	<Products>
		<Product>
			<Price>2.5</Price>
			<Rating>4</Rating>
			<DiscontinuedDate/>
			<ID>0</ID>
			<ReleaseDate>1992-01-01T00:00:00.000</ReleaseDate>
		</Product>
	</Products>
	<Categories>
		<Category>
			<ID>0</ID>
			<Name>Food</Name>
		</Category>
	</Categories>
	<Suppliers>
		<Supplier>
			<Address>
				<State>WA</State>
				<ZipCode>98074</ZipCode>
				<Street>NE 228th</Street>
				<Country>USA</Country>
				<City>Sammamish</City>
			</Address>
			<Concurrency>0</Concurrency>
			<ID>0</ID>
			<Name>Exotic Liquids</Name>
		</Supplier>
	</Suppliers>
	<Employee>
		<Name>Hirva</Name>
		<ID>4</ID>
	</Employee>
	<Book>
		<Book_ID>101</Book_ID>
		<Book_Title>Effective Java</Book_Title>
		<Book_Author>Joshua Bloch</Book_Author>
		<Book_Description>A comprehensive guide to best practices in Java programming.</Book_Description>
	</Book>
	<Customers>
		<Customer>
			<Number>1</Number>
			<FirstName>Fred</FirstName>
			<LastName>Landis</LastName>
			<Address>
				<Street>Oakstreet</Street>
				<City>Boston</City>
				<ZIP>23320</ZIP>
				<State>MA</State>
			</Address>
		</Customer>
	</Customers>
	<breakfast_menu>
		<food>
			<name>Belgian Waffles</name>
			<price>$5.95</price>
			<description>Two of our famous Belgian Waffles with plenty of real maple syrup</description>
			<calories>650</calories>
		</food>
	</breakfast_menu>
</Root>

Step 2: Send Data from Postman

  • Method: POST

  • URL: Your SAP CPI iFlow endpoint (e.g., https://<tenant>.iflmap.sap.hana.ondemand.com/http/canonicalEvent)

  • Header: Content-Type: application/xml

    Body: Paste the sample canonical message that in the step-1 given.

    Step 3: Build SAP CPI iFlow

    Component Purpose

    HTTPS Sender AdapterReceives HTTP POST from Postman
    Content Modifier (optional)Logs or adds initial data
    Router (Content-Based)Routes based on Type (SalesOrder, Product)
    JMS Receiver AdapterSends message to the target JMS queue (SalesOrderQueue, ProductQueue)
    parallel multicastAllows you to send the same message to multiple routes for parallel processing

    Router Conditions:

    If using XML XPath:

    • Condition 1 (Sales Order):
      //CanonicalMessage/Type = 'SalesOrder'

    • Condition 2 (Product):
      //CanonicalMessage/Type = 'Product'

      Step 4: Configure JMS Queues

      1.In SAP CPI Cockpit:

      • Go to JMS Resources

      • Create Queues:

        • SalesOrderQueue

        • ProductQueue

      2.In CPI iFlow:

      • Add JMS Receiver Adapter

      • Point each route to its respective queue.

      Step 5: Deploy & Test

      1.Deploy the iFlow.
      2.Post Data using Postman.
      3.Check Monitoring:

      • In SAP CPI Message Monitor, confirm successful routing.

      • In JMS Queue Monitor, confirm that the message lands in the correct queue.

      Error Handling: Sending Email on Exception


      Steps to Implement Email Notification in Error Handling:

      1. Add Exception Subprocess:
      In your main iFlow:

      • Drag and drop an Exception Subprocess (gray dotted box around your main flow).

      • This subprocess will automatically catch any unhandled exceptions in the main flow

        2.Add Error Handling Components:

         

      •  

        Component Purpose

        Start Event (Escalation)Starts the exception subprocess
        Content ModifierPrepares email content (subject, body, etc.)
        Mail Receiver AdapterSends the actual email

        3.Configure the Content Modifier (Prepare Email):

        Exchange Property:

        • EmailSubject: Error in CPI iFlow: Canonical Event Router

        • EmailBody: {As like you want}

          4. Configure Mail Receiver Adapter:

        • Protocol: Mail

        • SMTP Server: Your mail server (e.g., smtp.office365.com)

        • Authentication: Yes (configure in Security Materials)

        • To: e.g., support@yourcompany.com

        • Subject: ${property.EmailSubject}

        • Body: ${property.EmailBody}

        Make sure your CPI tenant has Mail (SMTP) connectivity set up.

        5. Deploy & Test:

        • Force an error: You can test by deliberately sending invalid data from Postman.

        • Check:

          • CPI Monitoring (Exception Subprocess should trigger)

          • Email inbox (verify error notification received) if any error occurs.

            The detailed error mail triggers like this if any error happens in the entire workflow and i used the HTML code in the mail triggering body to make email looks even more beautiful.

          • If Error doesn't occurs the data would goes to their respective JMS Queues.

entry.title: How to Configure and Perform Transport Checks in SAP CALM: SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition
entry.content:
Objective: This blog aims to guide users through the configuration and execution of transport checks in SAP Cloud ALM, with a specific focus on Downgrade Protection (DGP) and Cross-Reference Checks. These checks help ensure that transports do not unintentionally overwrite newer objects (DGP) and that all dependent or referenced objects are correctly included (Cross-Reference Checks), thereby maintaining system integrity and minimizing post-deployment issues in S/4HANA Private Cloud environments. This blog aims to guide users through the configuration and execution of transport checks in SAP Cloud ALM, with a specific focus on Downgrade Protection (DGP) and Cross-Reference Checks. These checks help ensure that transports do not unintentionally overwrite newer objects (DGP) and that all dependent or referenced objects are correctly included (Cross-Reference Checks), thereby maintaining system integrity and minimizing post-deployment issues in S/4HANA Private Cloud environments.

Transport checks is one of the new features that SAP CALM is providing.

The Transport Checks are available for Features application in Cloud ALM, and you can check a CTS-managed transport set to evaluate the impact on your production tenant.

The checks include Downgrade Protection and a Cross-Reference Check. It’s possible to choose to perform an individual check or both.

Downgrade Protection (DGP) tracks objects in transport requests, and reports conflicts when different versions of the same object that is saved in two or more transport requests is imported.

  • Predecessor Check (Sequence Violation)

The system can detect conflicting predecessors, that is, preceding transport requests containing conflicts, at the time of importing transport requests to the production.

  • Imminent Check (Downgrade Situation)

The system can detect impending (imminent) downgrade conflicts when transport requests are imported. This kind of conflict would become an actual downgrade if you ignored the conflict.

Cross-reference Check can detect inconsistencies for objects that are referenced in transport requests, indicating object dependencies.

Objects in transport requests sometimes refer to other objects that are not included in the transport. The cross-reference check applies a where-used-analysis to check the referenced objects in transport requests. If a referenced object doesn’t exist in the target system or if the referenced object in the source system has a different version than in the target system, the check indicates a potential error. In addition, the check shows the last transport requests for the missing object versions.

Let me take you through how to configure these transport checks and perform them in SAP CALM.
The first step is to enable the use case Transports: Check in Production system during the registration of the systems while configuring SAP CALM for Implementation:


To configure SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation for your SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, do check out my blog:
Re: How to Configure SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation for Your SAP S/4 HANA Private Cloud Landscape

Just enabling Transports: check is not enough to perform the checks; RFC destination needs to be maintained with the source tenant, if you do not maintain it, you will get the below error message:

To maintain the RFC destination, follow the below steps:
Step 1: Goto SAP Note: 2475591 and download the role: SAP_SDF_ALM_TRCHK.SAP from the Attachments section:

Now goto the Development system of your landscape and upload the downloaded role in the tcode PFCG-> Role->Upload:

Step 2: Now Goto tcode SU01 in the Development system of your landscape and create a user, for example: TRCHECK_CALM, give the initial password and give the same role that we uploaded in step 1 to this user and save the User.

Step 3: Now we need to create RFC in Development system of the landscape, to do so goto SM59 and click on create button:

Give the Destination name (For example: TRCHECK_CALM_RFC) and select the connection type as 3:

In the Technical settings tab give the target host and instance no:

In the logon & Security tab, give the user we created in Step 2 and the password and save the RFC destination:

Do the connection test and authorization test:

Step 4: Login to Production system 000 client in SM30 Table view: /SDF/CMO_TARGET and maintain the RFC destination just created in step 3:

Once all the steps are performed, login to SAP CALM tenant and click on perform all checks in the Feature when the Transports have target tenant as Production system:

The results for Transports check are then reflected:

After the transport checks are performed, you can now deploy your changes to Production system.
